FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (N=1985 adolescents, Year 2 mean age 11.91 years, followed to Year 4)

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use measured via Youth Screen Time Survey (self-reported)

FT_IC3.evidence: Prospective regression models testing associations between social media use and sleep duration, moderated by brain activation (fMRI during Monetary Incentive Delay task). Multiple tests across sleep→SM and SM→sleep pathways.

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract states 'Greater social media use at Year 2 predicted shorter sleep duration at Year 4' with effects reported, including moderation by specific brain regions (nucleus accumbens, cingulate gyrus, insula, putamen)

FT_IC5.evidence: Longitudinal empirical analysis examining prospective associations over two years with inferential tests and multiple outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article: Journal of Sleep Research, 2026, DOI 10.1111/jsr.70325

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes smartphone/SM as both IV (predicting sleep) and DV (predicted by sleep), plus includes individual psychological predictors (brain activation in reward/executive control regions), so FT-EC7 does not apply
notes: No PDF available; assessment based on abstract. Clear bidirectional relationship between SM use and sleep with neural moderation.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data (N=11,878 children, ages 9-10 at baseline)

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract mentions 'screen time use' examined as correlate of emotion dysregulation but does not specify whether this is smartphone-specific, social media-specific, or generic screen time composite. Insufficient detail to determine if meets FT-IC2.

FT_IC3.evidence: Abstract does not clearly describe the statistical tests linking screen time (if separately measured) to emotion dysregulation outcomes. Cannot verify inferential test structure.

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract focuses on psychiatric/psychosocial correlates with numeric results (aOR, p-values) but no specific numeric results for screen time associations are provided in abstract

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD Study data examining correlates of emotion dysregulation

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article: Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology, 2026, DOI 10.1177/10445463251415488

FT_EC7_reason: Cannot determine; insufficient information in abstract about exposure specification and outcome direction. Recommend PDF review to clarify screen time measurement specificity.
notes: No PDF available. Abstract mentions screen time but does not specify whether it is smartphone-specific, social media-specific, or generic STS composite (E18 concern). Requires full PDF review to determine if FT-IC2 is MET or NOT_MET and whether phone/SM variable is separately extractable.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data across three waves (Years 2, 3, 4) with N≈7,260 participants

FT_IC2.evidence: Primary exposure and outcome is Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), which measures gaming addiction. While video games can be played on smartphones, this paper focuses on gaming addiction without smartphone/social media as primary exposure. Does not meet definition of smartphone/SM-specific variable per protocol.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects regression models test longitudinal associations between ADHD diagnosis (predictor) and VGAQ scores (outcome) with parallel mediation model examining nine psychosocial mediators

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results reported: Children with ADHD had average increase of 1.3 points on VGAQ (p<0.001); three significant mediators identified with effect percentages (prosocial peer involvement 7.4%, school involvement 5.8%, family conflict 5.1%)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original longitudinal empirical analysis using linear mixed-effects models and parallel mediation analysis across three study waves

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article: Frontiers in Psychiatry, 2026, DOI 10.3389/fpsyt.2026.1756782

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable because exposure is ADHD diagnosis (individual psychological characteristic), not demographic-only predictor. Paper reports individual predictor (ADHD) of gaming addiction, which automatically qualifies per E16.
notes: EXCLUSION: Does not meet FT-IC2. Primary exposure/outcome is video gaming addiction measured by VGAQ, not smartphone use or social media use. While video games may sometimes be played on smartphones, the paper's focus is gaming disorder, not smartphone/SM exposure. This is a gaming addiction study, not a smartphone/SM study per protocol narrowing criteria.
